spring.title: Prune after flowering
spring.details: Trim back branches immediately after the spring bloom fades. Cut above a leaf node to shape. Avoid late-season pruning as it removes next year's flower buds.

summer.title: Water deeply
summer.details: Keep shallow roots evenly moist during dry July/August stretches to prevent leaf scorch and preserve next spring's developing buds.

spring.title: Dead inner wood removal
spring.details: Reach inside the dense, prickly canopy to clear out dead, brown needles and brittle twigs. This opens up airflow and keeps the inner center from rotting.

summer.title: Light tip control
summer.details: Gently snip back exceptionally long, stray branch tips if they disrupt the neat, low-mound shape. Avoid shearing across the foliage into old woody sections, as bare inner branches will never regenerate new needles.

spring.title: Cane renewal and thinning
spring.details: Prune during dormancy before buds open. Cut out dead, diseased, or low-spreading wood. Remove the oldest, thickest canes down to the base to stimulate productive young growth, and thin out weak, twiggy growth in the center to optimize fruit size.

summer.title: Light tipping and containment
summer.details: Trim back spent flower clusters and soft green growth to maintain a tight form. Do not cut back into old, thick wood that lacks leaves, as California Lilac cannot regenerate buds from bare wood and will leave permanent dead zones.

spring.title: Structural thinning and containment
spring.details: Remove dead wood or stray upward-growing branches to maintain a flat, low-profile groundcover habit. Can tolerate hard renewal pruning if it overgrows edges.

summer.title: Edging control
summer.details: Lightly snip back aggressive, creeping tips that spill onto walkways or crowd the sprinkler head.

spring.title: Dead wood removal
spring.details: Reach inside the dense canopy to cut out dead, brown inner branches. This improves airflow and reduces fungal risks in wet PNW weather.

summer.title: Light structural pruning
summer.details: Thin out competing or crowded outer branches to maintain a clean, natural form. Never cut past the green foliage into the bare dead zone of old wood, as it will not regenerate.

spring.title: Dead cane removal
spring.details: Look inside the compact mound and snip off any completely dried or dead canes at the soil line. Avoid formal shearing across the top, which ruins its naturally delicate, feathery fine texture.

summer.title: Hard rejuvenation shear
summer.details: Shear the entire plant down aggressively to about 2 inches from the ground once the primary flowering flush turns ragged and floppy. This triggers rapid, clean new leaf development and often a second flush of flowers by late summer.

spring.title: Shear after bloom
spring.details: Shear lightly with hedge trimmers immediately after winter or early spring flowers fade. Cut just below the spent flower spikes to keep the mound dense and tight. Do not cut into old, woody stems.

spring.title: Hard shearing
spring.details: Shear back the previous year's growth rigorously in March or April before new green growth starts. Cut right below old flower stalks to prevent the center from becoming leggy and bare.

spring.title: Selective cane thinning
spring.details: Prune out up to one-third of the oldest, tallest, or barest canes right at ground level to manage vertical height. This promotes dense foliage growth from the base and stops the plant from looking leggy or top-heavy. Avoid shearing the tops straight across.

spring.title: Winter damage cleanup
spring.details: Examine the succulent mats and clip out any mushy, blackened, or completely dead stems caused by winter frosts. Avoid heavy pruning; only remove compromised areas to give the plant room to naturally spread.

spring.title: Deadwood and crossing cleanup
spring.details: Prune right after the spring blossoms fade. Cut out dead, diseased, or crossing structural branches to open up light inside the canopy. Avoid fall or winter cuts to minimize susceptibility to fungal cankers.

spring.title: Containment pruning
spring.details: Prune back leading edges or overlapping layers to manage its footprint. Cut dead or bare trailing stems back to a healthy lateral branch.

spring.title: Winter cleanup and structural thinning
spring.details: Cut back dead wood down to the first pair of healthy, fat green buds. To manage overall bush density, cut up to one-third of the oldest, weakest canes completely down to the ground. Avoid pruning fresh stems entirely, as they carry the current season's blooms.

summer.title: Deadheading spent flowers
summer.details: Snip off old flower heads just below the bloom down to the next node if a tidier appearance is desired. Alternatively, leave the spent lacecap heads intact through winter to protect the lower nodes from severe cold.

spring.title: Structural shaping and containment
spring.details: Prune branches back to an outward-facing node to preserve a tight, clean rounded shape. If it has overgrown its space, it responds well to hard renewal cuts early in the season, though this will sacrifice the following winter's berry crop.

summer.title: Post-flowering hard shear
summer.details: Cut the entire low mat back by about half right as the blue flowers finish fading. This intense trim prevents the plant from developing a split, bare center and forces dense, fresh green foliage to ground level.

spring.title: Candle pruning
spring.details: Snap or cleanly cut the fresh upright 'candles' (the new green growth tips) in half before the needles unfurl. This curbs the annual expansion and forces a denser, compact canopy without inducing wood rot.

summer.title: Selective structural thinning
summer.details: Prune out dead, weak, or crossing inner branches to let sunlight penetrate the core. Always leave active green needles on any branch you trim; cutting back into bare, old wood will result in a permanent dead spot.

spring.title: Candle pruning
spring.details: Snap or cleanly cut the new upright 'candles' (the fresh spring growth tips) in half before the needles unroll. This limits annual branch expansion, thickens the canopy, and keeps the pine naturally small and dense without creating dead spots.

spring.title: Post-bloom structural thinning
spring.details: Prune immediately after the primary spring white flower flush fades. Cut back leggy branches to a strong lower leaf joint to keep the shrub compact. This timing permits the plant to develop new wood for late-season flowers.

summer.title: Stray cane reduction
summer.details: Snip off individual erratic, vertical shoots that poke out past the main rounded body of the shrub. Avoid severe fall pruning which leaves new growth vulnerable to early frost damage.

spring.title: Cane reduction after flowering
spring.details: Prune back the tallest canes to a lower outward-facing node right after the yellow spring flowers drop. If the plant becomes bare at the bottom, cut the oldest canes completely to the ground to spark fresh leafy growth from the base.

spring.title: Deadwood and crossing cleanup
spring.details: Prune right after the spring blossoms fade. Cut out dead, diseased, or crossing structural branches to open up light inside the canopy. Avoid fall or winter cuts to minimize susceptibility to fungal diseases.

spring.title: Hard framework pruning
spring.details: Prune back before green growth begins. Unlike macrophyllas, panicles bloom strictly on new wood. Cut branches back by one-half to two-thirds to a sturdy pair of buds to produce massive flower heads, and remove low, spindly interior stems entirely.

spring.title: Shape and thin
spring.details: Prune to maintain a tight, compact mound. Remove any dead internal twigs to open up light penetration. Thrives with early-season shaping.

summer.title: Light tipping
summer.details: Snip back errant, stray shoots that disrupt the clean spherical shape.

spring.title: Deadheading and structural pruning
spring.details: Snap off spent flower trusses carefully by hand right above the fresh leaf buds to redirect plant energy. For larger branches, cut back to a visible leaf node or growth whorl to thin out dead inner wood and maintain a balanced, natural shape without stripping lower foliage.

spring.title: Hard rejuvenation or structural thinning
spring.details: If overgrown, this shrub can be cut back hard within a few inches of the ground to reset its size. Otherwise, remove one-third of the oldest, thickest canes at the base to manage height and encourage vibrant new pink foliage.

summer.title: Whip containment
summer.details: Trim back long, aggressive vertical whips to keep the shrub from crowding paths or nearby plants.

spring.title: Candle reduction
spring.details: Snap the expanding new growth candles by one-third to one-half before the needles fully separate. This limits excessive branch extension and densifies the foliage crown.

summer.title: Structural canopy cleanup
summer.details: Prune out entirely dead twigs or crossing branches to display the tree's natural, asymmetrical architecture. Never chop into old, needle-free wood unless you intend to completely remove that entire branch.

spring.title: Hard rejuvenation cut
spring.details: Cut the entire shrub down sharply to about 4-6 inches from the ground. Spirea blooms on brand-new summer wood; this hard reset guarantees compact, non-woody growth and maximum flowering.

summer.title: Deadhead and shear spent blooms
summer.details: Shear off the first flush of faded flowers in mid-summer. This cleans up the brown seed heads and frequently triggers a second, lighter bloom cycle in late summer.

spring.title: Structural thinning and limb clearance
spring.details: Prune out dead branches or inner crossing limbs to emphasize its interesting, peeling bark profile. If growing near structures or paths, raise the canopy by removing lower lateral branches cleanly back to the main trunk.

summer.title: Deadheading and foliage clean up
summer.details: Cut down the spent purple spike stalks right to the basal leaf cluster once flowering completely finishes. This prevents seed production and keeps the crinkled green foliage mat tight and healthy through the season.

spring.title: Shape or hard renewal pruning
spring.details: Unlike most conifers, Yew handles heavy pruning remarkably well. Trim outer foliage back to manage its size or footprint. Old, leggy specimens can even be cut deep into old wood if a size reset is required, as it readily sprouts fresh growth from bare branches.

summer.title: Light canopy neatening
summer.details: Trim away any erratic, rapid growth shoots that break the clean structural lines before the plant halts active development for winter.
